MASTER CALENDAR COURT

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Oct-27-2021 Continued To Dec-22-21 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Oct-05-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
Constantin Roboostoff, Esq. (415.732.0282; cvrl@earthlink.net) of Roboostoff & Kalkin for Plaintiff.
James Ellison, Esq. (415.986.5900; jlellison@grsm.com) of Gordon Rees Scully Mansukhani, LLP for Defendants.

ARGUED (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Defendant Mercury Insurance Group's ("MIG") demurrer to Plaintiff's First Amended Complaint ("FAC") is overruled in its entirety. Taking all allegations of the FAC as true, Plaintiff alleges facts sufficient to constitute a cause of action for breach of contract and the covenant of good faith and fair dealing.

Defendant contends it is not a party to the California Automobile Insurance Company Policy ("CAIC Policy") between Plaintiffs and CAIC such that MIG could be held liable for breach of contract or breach of the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. (Altura Decl. 12, Ex. K.) Plaintiff alleges MIG breached its obligations under the terms of the CAIC Policy by refusing to provide coverage for property damage, as well as refusing to indemnify Plaintiff in third party claims. (FAC 3, 4, and 5.) Although MIG is not a party to the CAIC Policy, Plaintiff contends CAIC is a wholly owned subsidiary of MIG. (FAC 3.) Plaintiff further alleges "MIG acted as the administrative agent during the review and denial of Plaintiff's claims under the policy and exercised authority and control over the payment of benefits under the policy." (FAC 5.) Under California law, a parent may be liable for a subsidiary's conduct where the parent's control is "so pervasive and continual that the subsidiary may be considered nothing more than an agent or instrumentality of the parent." (See Sonora Diamond Corp. v. Superior Court (2000) 83 Cal.App.4th 523, 541.) At this stage of the proceedings, these factual allegations must be treated as true and are sufficient to plead the existence and breach of a contract between the parties. (See Berkley v. Dowds (2007) 152 Cal.App.4th 518, 525.)

The Court rejects MIG's argument Plaintiff's FAC is a sham pleading. "Under the sham pleading doctrine, plaintiffs are precluded from amending complaints to omit harmful allegations, without explanation, from previous complaints to avoid attacks raised in demurrers or motions for summary judgment." (Deveny v. Entropin, Inc. (2006) 139 Cal.App.4th 408, 425.) "The sham pleading doctrine is not 'intended to prevent honest complainants from correcting erroneous allegations ... or to prevent correction of ambiguous facts.' Instead, it is intended to enable courts 'to prevent an abuse of process.'" (Id. at 426.) In this case, Plaintiff explained the supposed contradiction between the complaint and amended complaint was due to a misunderstanding regarding the relationship between MIC and CAIC. (Roboostoff Decl. Ex. A.) Plaintiffs are not attempting to omit harmful facts, rather Plaintiff's present contention is that CAIC is a wholly owned subsidiary of MIG. Subsequent proceedings will resolve that contested factual issue.

Order is signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: S. Le, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)
